subtopic_detail: Vitamin B12 deficiency with neurological features
stem: A 64-year-old woman presents to her GP with 3 months of progressive fatigue, paraesthesiae of the toes and increasing unsteadiness when walking in the dark. She has type 1 diabetes on insulin and a 20-year history of metformin use was stopped earlier this year. She has no history of pernicious anaemia, no recent bowel surgery and no vegan dietary restriction. She has no recent change in medications and no history of alcohol misuse documented in her file.

data_table.Examination: Reduced vibration sense at the great toes bilaterally; impaired joint position sense; positive Romberg; normal cranial nerves; brisk knee reflexes with absent ankle reflexes
data_table.Investigations: Hb 102 g/L, MCV 108 fL, platelets 220 x10^9/L, WCC 4.6 x10^9/L; serum B12 78 pmol/L (reference > 200 pmol/L); folate 14 nmol/L; TSH 1.4 mU/L; anti-intrinsic factor antibody positive

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial treatment?

options.text: Intramuscular hydroxocobalamin 1000 micrograms every other day for 1 to 2 weeks until neurological progression halts, then maintenance every 3 months for life
options.rationale: Pernicious anaemia with neurological involvement is treated with IM hydroxocobalamin loading then 3-monthly maintenance for life. (Source: AMH: hydroxocobalamin)

options.text: Oral cyanocobalamin 1 mg daily for life without intramuscular loading therapy as the first-line treatment of vitamin B12 deficiency with neurological signs in this Australian outpatient
options.rationale: Oral high-dose B12 is acceptable in dietary deficiency without neurological features; AU practice prefers IM loading for neurological involvement. (Source: AMH: hydroxocobalamin)

options.text: Oral folic acid 5 mg daily for 4 months alone, repeating macrocytic anaemia indices in 4 to 6 weeks, with no B12 supplementation initiated in this presentation in primary care today
options.rationale: Giving folate alone can precipitate or worsen subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord. (Source: AMH: folic acid)

options.text: Pack-red-cell transfusion of 2 units in the day ward as the first-line therapy for symptomatic macrocytic anaemia at a haemoglobin of 102 g/L in this older woman today in the GP rooms
options.rationale: Hb 102 with stable cardiorespiratory status does not warrant transfusion; correction of the deficiency restores haematopoiesis. (Source: NBA Patient Blood Management)

options.text: Recommence metformin 500 mg twice daily and add iron polymaltose 105 mg once daily to address the macrocytic anaemia she has developed since stopping metformin earlier this year today
options.rationale: Metformin causes B12 deficiency, not macrocytic anaemia from its withdrawal; iron addresses microcytic, not macrocytic anaemia. (Source: AMH: metformin and B12)

explanation.summary: Vitamin B12 deficiency with neurological features (subacute combined degeneration of the dorsal columns) requires intramuscular hydroxocobalamin loading followed by lifelong maintenance every 3 months in pernicious anaemia. Oral high-dose cyanocobalamin is acceptable in dietary deficiency without neurological involvement. Folate must never precede B12 replacement in macrocytic anaemia because of precipitation risk. Metformin and PPIs are common contributors. Anti-intrinsic factor and anti-parietal cell antibodies support pernicious anaemia.
key_points: IM hydroxocobalamin loading is preferred in B12 deficiency with neurological features
Never give folate before B12 in macrocytic anaemia because of SCDC precipitation risk
Metformin and PPIs are common contributors to B12 deficiency
explanation.pearls: Hydroxocobalamin 1000 micrograms intramuscular every 2 to 3 days for 2 weeks then 3-monthly for life is the AU regimen for pernicious anaemia with neurological involvement.

stem: A 65-year-old woman with newly diagnosed metastatic breast cancer presents to ED with 5 days of confusion, polyuria, polydipsia and constipation. She is no longer eating or drinking well. She has no chronic kidney disease, no current bisphosphonate use, no known QT prolongation, no atrial fibrillation, and is not currently on diuretics. Her dental review for jaw clearance was unremarkable last month, and there is no evidence of osteonecrosis of the jaw.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 112/68, HR 102, RR 18, Temp 36.7 degrees Celsius, SpO2 98% on room air
data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, GCS 14 (confused), no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: Corrected calcium 3.4 mmol/L, phosphate 0.7 mmol/L, albumin 32 g/L, creatinine 124 micromol/L (baseline 78), Mg 0.78 mmol/L; PTH suppressed; PTHrP elevated; ECG QTc 380 ms

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial management plan?

options.text: Intravenous frusemide 40 mg eight hourly without prior fluid resuscitation, with hourly monitoring of urine output and serum electrolytes in this woman to forcibly diurese calcium quickly
options.rationale: Loop diuretics are added only after adequate volume repletion in normal renal function; using them first worsens dehydration and hypercalcaemia. (Source: eTG Endocrinology: hypercalcaemia)

options.text: Aggressive intravenous saline rehydration with 3 to 4 litres in the first 24 hours and intravenous zoledronic acid 4 mg once renal function permits, plus calcitonin if symptomatic in the meantime
options.rationale: Volume repletion with saline is the first-line move; IV zoledronic acid provides sustained calcium lowering once renal function is restored; calcitonin offers a rapid bridging effect. (Source: eTG Endocrinology: hypercalcaemia)

options.text: Urgent haemodialysis as the first intervention before any intravenous fluid challenge or pharmacological calcium-lowering therapy is administered in this hypercalcaemia of malignancy presentation today
options.rationale: Dialysis is reserved for severe symptomatic hypercalcaemia with renal failure not responsive to fluids and pharmacology; not first-line. (Source: eTG Endocrinology: hypercalcaemia)

options.text: Subcutaneous denosumab 120 mg as the immediate first-line therapy for hypercalcaemia of malignancy in this woman with otherwise normal renal function and no prior antiresorptive therapy
options.rationale: Denosumab is used when bisphosphonates fail or are contraindicated by renal failure; it is not first-line monotherapy in initial hypercalcaemia. (Source: eTG Endocrinology: hypercalcaemia)

options.text: Oral cinacalcet 30 mg twice daily commenced from admission as the immediate first-line treatment for hypercalcaemia of malignancy in this woman before any intravenous saline rehydration begins
options.rationale: Cinacalcet is used in parathyroid carcinoma and tertiary hyperparathyroidism, not as first-line for hypercalcaemia of malignancy. (Source: eTG Endocrinology: hypercalcaemia)

explanation.summary: Hypercalcaemia of malignancy requires aggressive intravenous saline rehydration (3 to 4 litres over 24 hours), correction of magnesium and potassium, and a bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid 4 mg IV) once renal function permits. Calcitonin (4 IU/kg 12-hourly) gives faster but short-lived calcium lowering as a bridge in symptomatic patients. Denosumab is used when bisphosphonates fail or in renal failure. Loop diuretics are added only after volume repletion in normal renal function.
key_points: Saline rehydration precedes any pharmacological calcium-lowering therapy
Zoledronic acid 4 mg IV gives sustained calcium lowering within 48 hours
Calcitonin acts within hours but with tachyphylaxis within 48 hours
explanation.pearls: Zoledronic acid 4 mg IV lowers calcium across 48 to 96 hours with effects lasting around 4 weeks; calcitonin 4 IU/kg subcutaneously every 12 hours bridges that delay but loses effect within 48 hours.

stem: A 34-year-old man presents to ED with acute swelling of the lips and tongue and a hoarse voice that started 90 minutes ago. He has had four similar previous episodes of non-pruritic, non-urticarial facial swelling, two with abdominal cramping pain that required admission. His father has the same problem. He is not on any new medications, has had no insect stings, no new foods and no contact with latex. Standard intramuscular adrenaline and intravenous antihistamines have had minimal effect.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 132/82, HR 102, RR 22, SpO2 96% on 4 L nasal prongs, Temp 36.8 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: Tongue and lip oedema, hoarse voice, no urticaria, no wheeze, no hypotension
data_table.Investigations: Tryptase pending; previous serology: C4 0.04 g/L (low), C1 esterase inhibitor functional activity 22 percent of normal (low) on stored bloods; FBC and UEC normal today

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next therapeutic agent?

options.text: A further dose of intramuscular adrenaline 500 micrograms with an intravenous adrenaline infusion at low dose as the priority therapy for refractory angioedema in this patient
options.rationale: Adrenaline targets histamine-mediated anaphylaxis; bradykinin-mediated HAE does not respond to adrenaline, antihistamines or corticosteroids. (Source: ASCIA HAE guidance)

options.text: Intravenous hydrocortisone 200 mg, intravenous chlorphenamine 10 mg, and nebulised salbutamol 5 mg as the priority therapy for this severe refractory angioedema episode in the emergency department today
options.rationale: Corticosteroids, antihistamines and beta agonists do not work in bradykinin-mediated HAE; targeted C1-INH replacement or bradykinin pathway inhibition is required. (Source: ASCIA HAE guidance)

options.text: Intravenous C1 esterase inhibitor concentrate (20 IU/kg) or subcutaneous icatibant 30 mg as targeted bradykinin pathway therapy with airway-ready ENT and anaesthetic backup
options.rationale: HAE laryngeal attacks require C1-INH concentrate or icatibant as first-line; ASCIA recommends airway-ready monitoring. (Source: ASCIA HAE guidance)

options.text: Oral danazol 200 mg three times daily commenced now as acute therapy for this hereditary angioedema attack, with intravenous antihistamines and observation in the short stay unit afterwards
options.rationale: Danazol is long-term prophylaxis in HAE, not acute attack therapy; onset is far too slow for laryngeal involvement. (Source: ASCIA HAE guidance)

options.text: Endotracheal intubation under general anaesthesia and observation in the intensive care unit without administering any specific pharmacological angioedema-directed therapy in the meantime
options.rationale: Airway management plans must be in place, but specific HAE therapy is not delayed; C1-INH or icatibant is given concurrently with airway preparation. (Source: ASCIA HAE guidance)

explanation.summary: Hereditary angioedema is bradykinin-mediated and does not respond to adrenaline, antihistamines or corticosteroids. Acute attacks (especially laryngeal) are treated with C1 esterase inhibitor concentrate, icatibant (bradykinin B2-receptor antagonist) or ecallantide (kallikrein inhibitor), alongside airway preparedness. Long-term prophylaxis options include attenuated androgens (danazol), tranexamic acid, lanadelumab, and prophylactic C1-INH. C4 is a useful screening test in steady state.
key_points: HAE does not respond to adrenaline, antihistamines or corticosteroids
Acute therapy is C1-INH concentrate or icatibant alongside airway preparedness
Danazol is long-term prophylaxis, not acute attack therapy
explanation.pearls: A persistently low C4 between attacks is the most useful screening test for hereditary angioedema and prompts confirmatory C1 esterase inhibitor antigenic and functional assays.

stem: A 48-year-old woman presents to ED with episodic palpitations, sweating and severe headaches over 6 months, today with BP 224/128, headache and chest pain. Imaging in the last week showed a 4 cm right adrenal lesion with characteristic features on MRI. 24-hour fractionated urinary metanephrines were elevated at 5 times the upper limit of normal. She has no asthma, no diabetes, no current beta blocker and no contraindications to alpha or beta blockade. She is haemodynamically tachycardic in ED.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 224/128, HR 124, RR 18, Temp 36.7 degrees Celsius, SpO2 98% on room air
data_table.Examination: Pale, sweating, no focal neurological deficit, no signs of heart failure
data_table.Investigations: Troponin negative, ECG sinus tachycardia, CT brain normal; 24-hour urinary fractionated metanephrines 5 x ULN one week ago

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next pharmacological priority?

options.text: Intravenous metoprolol 5 mg increments as the priority agent to immediately bring the resting heart rate to under 80 in this woman with a hypertensive crisis presentation today
options.rationale: Beta blockade before adequate alpha blockade causes unopposed alpha vasoconstriction and worsens crisis; avoid until alpha-blocked. (Source: AMH: phenoxybenzamine; eTG Endocrinology: phaeochromocytoma)

options.text: Sublingual nifedipine 10 mg as the priority agent to rapidly drop blood pressure into the normotensive range in this woman with hypertensive crisis in the emergency department in front of you
options.rationale: Short-acting sublingual nifedipine causes rapid swings and reflex tachycardia and is not preferred; alpha blockade is the targeted therapy. (Source: AMH: nifedipine)

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 4 g bolus then 1 g per hour as the first-line agent to provide vasodilation and antiarrhythmic effect in this woman with severe catecholamine excess and hypertension today
options.rationale: Magnesium is an adjunct in severe phaeochromocytoma crisis but is not first-line monotherapy; alpha blockade is the pillar. (Source: eTG Endocrinology: phaeochromocytoma)

options.text: Commence alpha blockade with intravenous phentolamine boluses now and transition to oral phenoxybenzamine over the next 7 to 14 days before adding any beta blocker for sinus tachycardia
options.rationale: Phaeochromocytoma management requires alpha blockade first; beta blockade is added only after alpha blockade is established to avoid unopposed alpha-mediated hypertensive crisis. (Source: eTG Endocrinology: phaeochromocytoma)

options.text: Refer for urgent laparoscopic adrenalectomy within 24 hours without any pharmacological optimisation in this woman with biochemically confirmed phaeochromocytoma and current hypertensive crisis
options.rationale: Adrenalectomy without 7 to 14 days of alpha blockade and 2 to 3 days of beta blockade is associated with severe intraoperative crisis and mortality. (Source: eTG Endocrinology: phaeochromocytoma)

explanation.summary: Phaeochromocytoma crisis is managed with alpha blockade first (intravenous phentolamine in crisis, oral phenoxybenzamine for at least 7 to 14 days preoperatively), followed by beta blockade for tachycardia once alpha blockade is established. Beta blockade alone causes unopposed alpha vasoconstriction and worsens hypertension. Adequate salt and fluid loading is required during alpha blockade. Surgery is planned after biochemical and haemodynamic optimisation.
key_points: Alpha blockade precedes beta blockade by 7 to 14 days preoperatively in phaeochromocytoma
Beta blockade before alpha blockade causes unopposed alpha vasoconstriction
Salt and fluid loading during alpha blockade supports intraoperative haemodynamics
explanation.pearls: Phenoxybenzamine 10 mg twice daily titrated upward over 7 to 14 days is the standard AU preoperative regimen, with adequate salt and fluid intake to limit postoperative hypotension.

stem: A 9-month-old previously well infant is brought to a regional ED with 3 days of coryza and 1 day of increasing cough and noisy breathing. He has not been feeding well today and his nappies are dry over the past 8 hours. He has had no apnoeas reported by his mother and is alert when stimulated. He is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander and is up to date with vaccinations. His chest auscultation reveals widespread fine crackles and mild expiratory wheeze.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 37.6 degrees Celsius, HR 158, RR 64, SpO2 90% on room air, capillary refill 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Moderate intercostal and subcostal recession, nasal flaring; cool peripheries; chest with widespread crackles and wheeze; abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: Point-of-care viral PCR positive for respiratory syncytial virus; capillary blood gas: pH 7.36, pCO2 42 mmHg, HCO3 22 mmol/L

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Admit, commence humidified low-flow nasal cannula oxygen to maintain SpO2 above 92 percent, support feeding with small frequent volumes or nasogastric feeds as needed, and reassess hourly
options.rationale: AU guidance targets SpO2 of at least 90 percent (not 92 percent); the higher threshold prolongs admission without outcome benefit and misstates AU policy. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Admit for trial of nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg every 20 minutes and intravenous hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg six hourly as the first-line therapy for moderate bronchiolitis in this child today
options.rationale: Bronchodilators and corticosteroids are not recommended in bronchiolitis under 12 months; supportive care is the mainstay. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Discharge home with parental safety-net advice, oral paracetamol 15 mg/kg six hourly and review with the GP in 24 hours if symptoms have not improved at all by then with rest
options.rationale: Hypoxia, dehydration and moderate work of breathing meet admission criteria; discharge home is unsafe. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence intravenous ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg once daily and order a chest X-ray as the first-line management for moderate bronchiolitis in this previously well 9-month-old infant today
options.rationale: Antibiotics and chest X-ray are not routinely indicated in straightforward viral bronchiolitis; supportive care is the mainstay. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Admit, commence humidified nasal cannula oxygen to keep SpO2 of at least 90 percent, support hydration with small frequent feeds or nasogastric top-ups, and reassess hourly
options.rationale: AU and RCH guidance for moderate bronchiolitis with hypoxia (SpO2 under 92 percent) and feeding difficulty is admission, oxygen to keep SpO2 of at least 90 percent, and feeding support; no routine antibiotics, bronchodilators or steroids. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis is a clinical diagnosis in children under 12 months. Admission is indicated for SpO2 under 92 percent on room air, moderate or severe work of breathing, feeding under 50 percent of usual, dehydration, apnoeas or risk factors such as ex-prematurity. Treatment is supportive: oxygen to maintain SpO2 of at least 90 percent, small frequent feeds or NG hydration, and humidified high-flow if escalation is needed. Bronchodilators, corticosteroids, antibiotics and chest X-rays are not routine.
key_points: Admit if SpO2 under 92 percent or moderate-severe work of breathing or feeding under 50 percent of usual
Oxygen target is SpO2 of at least 90 percent, not higher, in stable bronchiolitis
No routine bronchodilators, corticosteroids, antibiotics or chest X-rays
explanation.pearls: Oxygen saturation target of at least 90 percent (not 92 percent) is the AU paediatric bronchiolitis admission threshold; aiming higher prolongs admission without outcome benefit.
